texto: [L]ike people, ideas have social lives. They’re one way when they’re by themselves, and another when they’re surrounded by their peers. Crammed together, they grow more uncertain, more interesting, more surprising; they come out of themselves and grow more appealing, and funnier. You wouldn’t want all of intellectual life to be that social--we couldn’t make progress that way. But there’s a special atmosphere that develops whenever truly different ideas congregate, and, on the whole, it’s too rare.
explicacao: Orientação direta de Joshua Rothman sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. A afirmação “[L]ike people, ideas have social lives. They’re one way when they’re by themselves, and another w…” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a vida, cross-pollination, ideas, útil em momentos de ideas e innovation.

Crammed together, they grow more uncertain, more interesting, more surprising; they come out of themselves and grow more appealing, and funnier. But there’s a special atmosphere that develops whenever truly different ideas congregate, and, on the whole, it’s too rare.

texto: One of the ironies of college is that the impossibility of reading your way out of the modern predicament is something you learn about, as a student, by reading. Part of the value of a humanistic education has to do with a consciousness of, and a familiarity with, the limits that you’ll spend the rest of your life talking about and pushing against.
